title: Synthetic Datasets
lesson:
A synthetic dataset is training data that was made by a machine instead of collected from humans. In practice that almost always means one thing: you use a strong existing model (a big closed model, or a capable open one) to generate the examples you will train a smaller model on.

The word synthetic just means "manufactured" — like synthetic fabric is cloth made in a factory rather than picked from a cotton field. The examples are real text; they were simply written by an AI, not a person.

Why bother?

Because good human data is slow and expensive. Writing 50,000 high-quality SFT examples by hand could take a team weeks and cost a fortune. A model can draft those same 50,000 in an afternoon for the price of some API calls.

As of 2026 this is no longer a fringe trick. Most modern instruction-tuned models lean heavily on synthetic data, and many of the best open datasets are partly or wholly machine-made.

The master-chef analogy

Picture a world-class chef (the strong "teacher" model). You can't afford to hire them full-time, but you can film them cooking 10,000 dishes. Then you train a junior cook (your small "student" model) to copy those recordings until they can reproduce the dishes alone.

This is called distillation — squeezing the skill of a big, expensive model into a small, cheap one through the examples it produces. The catch: the student can only get as good as the recordings. Film the chef on a bad day and you teach the junior cook the chef's mistakes.

How it is actually done

The core loop is simple:

  • Write a prompt that asks the teacher to produce one training example.
  • Save its answer in the right shape (see Dataset Formatting).
  • Vary a seed — a topic, persona, or difficulty — on every call, so you get variety instead of 10,000 near-identical rows.

A well-known recipe called Self-Instruct does exactly this: start with a handful of human-written seed tasks, then ask the model to invent thousands of new tasks like them.

import random
topics = ["refunds", "shipping", "account login", "billing"]
tones  = ["frustrated", "polite", "confused"]

prompt = f"""Write one realistic customer-support exchange.
Topic: {random.choice(topics)}
Customer tone: {random.choice(tones)}
Return JSON: {{\"user\": ..., \"assistant\": ...}}"""
# call your teacher model with `prompt`, parse the JSON, append to train.jsonl

Run that thousands of times with different seeds and you have a varied dataset. The same idea powers Preference Datasets: ask the teacher to write two answers, then have a model judge which is better, giving you cheap "chosen vs rejected" pairs for DPO.

The big danger: model collapse

Here is the trap that catches beginners. If you train a model only on the slightly-flawed output of another model, errors compound. Imagine a photocopy of a photocopy of a photocopy: each generation looks fine on its own, but after many rounds the text turns to mush.

Researchers call this model collapse — quality and diversity quietly drain away as machine-made data trains the next machine, which makes more machine-made data, and so on. Synthetic data is a powerful seasoning, not the whole meal. The teams that win mix synthetic data with some real human data and filter aggressively.

💡 The reason synthetic data works at all is that for many tasks, checking an answer is easier than writing one. Maths can be re-computed, code can be run against tests, JSON can be validated. So the pro move is: generate a lot, then keep only what passes a hard check.

That filtering step is everything, and it ties directly to Data Curation and Dataset Cleaning — generation is the easy half; curation is where the quality comes from.

Human-collected dataSynthetic data
Slow, expensive to gatherFast, cheap to generate at scale
Naturally diverse and groundedCan be repetitive; risks model collapse
Privacy and licensing headachesCan avoid real user data entirely
Hard to cover rare edge casesEasy to target rare cases on demand

One honest caveat: a student trained on a teacher's output is capped by the teacher. It rarely beats the teacher at the teacher's own game — it just gets cheaper and faster at it. And check the teacher's terms of service: some commercial models forbid using their outputs to train competing models.

Treat the machine as a fast first-draft writer whose work you still ruthlessly edit, and synthetic data becomes one of the most leveraged moves in modern model building.
mentalModel: A photocopier. A strong teacher model is the original document; synthetic data is the copies it produces to train a cheaper student. Each copy is fine on its own — but copy a copy of a copy with no human checking in between, and the text slowly turns to mush (model collapse).
mistakes: Treating synthetic data as a free lunch. Generation is cheap, but good synthetic data isn't — the value comes from filtering. Generate 10x what you need and throw most of it away. Skipping the filter is the number-one rookie error.

Letting model collapse creep in. Training repeatedly on machine-made data with no real human data in the mix erodes diversity and quality, like photocopying a photocopy. Always anchor with some real examples and watch for output that gets blander every round.

Low diversity from lazy prompting. Calling the teacher with the same prompt 5,000 times yields 5,000 near-clones. Vary seeds — topics, personas, difficulty, length — or your model learns one narrow trick. See Instruction Tuning.

Generating tasks you can't verify. Synthetic data shines when checking is cheaper than writing (maths, code, structured JSON). For open-ended opinions there's no easy check, so flaws sneak through. Pick tasks with a clear correctness test where you can.

Inheriting the teacher's hidden flaws. The student copies the teacher's biases, refusals, formatting quirks, and confident wrong answers. Garbage in, garbage out — your data is only as good as the model that wrote it, so spot-check it like real hallucinations.

Ignoring the teacher's terms of service. Some commercial models legally forbid using their outputs to train other models. Check the licence before you ship a product on top of distilled data, or you may have to rebuild from a permissibly-licensed model.
exercise:
Build a small synthetic dataset and feel the filtering step. Pick a verifiable task — say, two-number addition. Programmatically (or by prompting a model) generate 100 examples in the shape {"user": "What is 47 + 86?", "assistant": "133"}. Now write a tiny checker that recomputes each sum and deletes any row where the assistant's answer is wrong. Count how many survive. You have just done generation plus curation — the two halves of every real synthetic-data pipeline.

Stretch goal: Add real diversity. Vary the number ranges (single digits up to 4-digit numbers) and the phrasing ("What is X plus Y?", "Add X and Y", "X + Y = ?") using a seed list, then de-duplicate the final set so no two rows are identical. Report how many of your 100 were unique and correct.
solution: Hint: The checker is the whole point. In Python: a, b = parse_numbers(row["user"]); keep = (str(a + b) == row["assistant"].strip()). Generating 100 and keeping only the correct ones is exactly how teams turn a noisy teacher into a clean training set — the model is allowed to be wrong, because the verifier catches it. For the stretch goal, build your prompts from a seed list (ranges, phrasings) and pick randomly each time; then de-duplicate with a set() of the user strings. You'll usually find a chunk were duplicates or wrong — which is precisely why you generate far more than you need and let the filter decide what survives.
summary: A synthetic dataset is training data generated by a strong "teacher" model rather than collected from humans — fast, cheap, and easy to target at rare cases. It powers distillation: squeezing a big model's skill into a small one. The catch is model collapse (quality drains when you train machine on machine), so always mix in real data, vary your prompts for diversity, and filter hard — generation is easy, curation is where the quality lives.
takeaways: Synthetic data is examples written by a strong teacher model, not humans.
It powers distillation: a small student copies a big teacher cheaply.
Generate lots, then filter hard — or risk model collapse.

diagram.caption: A teacher model plus varied seeds produces many raw examples; a hard verifier filters them, and the kept rows (anchored with some real data) train the student.

quiz.q: What does 'synthetic dataset' actually mean in LLM training?
options: Training data generated by a model rather than collected from humans
Fake data with no real text in it
Data that has been encrypted for privacy
A dataset stored in a synthetic file format
quiz.explain: Synthetic just means manufactured: the examples are real text, but a model wrote them instead of a person. It is the opposite of human-collected data, not encrypted or fake.

quiz.q: You distil a small student model from a strong teacher. What is the realistic ceiling on the student's skill?
options: It will reliably outperform the teacher because it is newer
It is roughly capped by the teacher — usually cheaper and faster, not better
It has no ceiling as long as you generate enough data
It can only ever reach 50% of the teacher's quality
quiz.explain: A student learns from the teacher's outputs, so it rarely beats the teacher at its own game. The win is cost and speed, not raw capability beyond the source.

quiz.q: Why is verification the secret weapon that makes synthetic data work for tasks like maths or code?
options: Verifying makes the model train faster
Checking an answer is often far cheaper than writing one, so you can filter out wrong rows
Verification removes the need for any human data
It encrypts the dataset against model collapse
quiz.explain: Re-running arithmetic or a test suite is cheap, so you can generate noisy output and keep only what passes. Open-ended tasks lack such a cheap check, which is why they're riskier.

quiz.q: A team trains each new model purely on the previous model's output, round after round, with no human data. What is the likely result?
options: The model keeps improving because each round is cleaner
Nothing changes; output stays identical forever
Model collapse — diversity and quality slowly drain away
The dataset gets larger but stays equally diverse
quiz.explain: Training machine output on machine output compounds small errors, like a photocopy of a photocopy. Mixing in real human data and filtering hard is what guards against this collapse.

keyTerms.def: Training data created by a machine (usually a strong model) instead of collected from humans.
Transferring a big "teacher" model's skill into a smaller "student" model by training the student on the teacher's outputs.
The teacher is the strong model that generates examples; the student is the smaller, cheaper model trained to copy them.
A recipe where you give a model a few human-written seed tasks and ask it to invent many more like them, bootstrapping a large dataset.
The slow loss of quality and diversity when models are trained on machine-made data over and over, like a photocopy of a photocopy.
A varying input (topic, persona, difficulty, phrasing) you change on each generation call to force diversity into the dataset.
Keeping only generated examples that pass a hard check. Works because checking an answer is often cheaper than writing one.
How varied the examples are. Low diversity (near-identical rows) teaches the model one narrow trick instead of the real range of inputs.
